1: וְאַבְרָהָם זָקֵן בָּא בַּיָּמִים (wĕʾaḇrāhām zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm) — "Abraham was old, coming in days." The formula zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm (old, entering in days) is the standard OT idiom for extreme old age (cf. 18:11 where the same phrase precedes the impossible-birth announcement for Sarah; Joshua 13:1; 23:1 for Joshua; 1 Kings 1:1 for David). The urgency it creates is narrative: Abraham must secure the covenant heir's marriage before he dies. וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם בַּכֹּל (the LORD had blessed Abraham in everything) — bakkōl (in everything, in all things) — comprehensive blessing without qualification; the single word encapsulates the fulfillment of the twelve-chapter promise arc from Genesis 12:2-3.
2: שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי (śîm-nāʾ yāḏĕḵā taḥat yĕrēḵî) — "place your hand under my thigh." The oath gesture of placing the hand under the thigh (yāreḵ) appears in the OT only here and Genesis 47:29 (Jacob's deathbed oath to Joseph). Yāreḵ (thigh, loins) is the seat of generative power and offspring (the "loins" from which children come: cf. Genesis 46:26; Exodus 1:5). Swearing by the thigh of the patriarch is an oath sworn by the covenant seed — the very offspring at stake in the promise. The servant is being bound by the most solemn form of covenant oath available in the patriarchal household: one that invokes the covenant line itself as guarantor.
3: הִשְׁבַּעְתִּיךָ בַּיהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ — "I make you swear by the LORD, God of heaven and God of earth." The double divine title — God of heaven and God of earth — emphasizes YHWH's universal sovereignty beyond the local land claim; it is an expansion of the usual "God of heaven" (Genesis 17:3; Ezra 1:2). אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי — the prohibition is absolute: lōʾ tiqqaḥ (you will not take) in the Qal imperfect with lōʾ = categorical prohibition. The Canaanite women are excluded categorically, not selectively — the covenant family must remain distinct from the people of the land.
4: כִּי אֶל-אַרְצִי וְאֶל-מוֹלַדְתִּי תֵּלֵךְ — "but to my land and to my birthplace you will go." ʾArṣî (my land) and môlaḏtî (my birthplace, kindred, origin) — exactly the terms Abraham left behind in Genesis 12:1 (ʾarṣĕḵā ûmimmôlaḏtĕḵā, your land and your birthplace), now reversed: the servant must go back to the origin-place while Abraham and Isaac remain in Canaan. The covenant heir cannot return to Mesopotamia (Genesis 24:6-8 make this explicit); only the proxy travels there. The covenant family remains in the promised land even while the covenant line is extended through a wife from the family of origin.
5: The servant's hypothetical question — "what if the woman refuses to come?" — is the practical obstacle to the mission and frames the conditional clauses that follow. ʾûlay (perhaps, what if) — the uncertainty introduced shows the servant's responsible planning; he is not asking for permission to fail but requesting contingency instructions. The question also sets up Abraham's response in Genesis 24:6-8, which is not a practical workaround but a theological declaration: the mission's success depends on YHWH's angelic provision, not on the servant's human contingencies.
6: הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן-תָּשִׁיב אֶת-בְּנִי שָׁמָּה — "take care for yourself, lest you take my son back there." Hišāmer lĕḵā (take care for yourself, guard yourself) — the Niphal imperative of šāmar is a strong warning; the reflexive form intensifies personal responsibility. The prohibition against returning Isaac to Mesopotamia is absolute — Abraham foresees the danger: once in the home country, the pull of family and familiarity might overwhelm the covenant calling to remain in Canaan. Isaac staying in the promised land is not negotiable; the woman coming to Isaac is the only acceptable scenario.
7: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם אֲשֶׁר לְקָחַנִי מִבֵּית אָבִי — "the LORD, God of heaven, who took me from my father's house." Abraham's retrospective summary: YHWH is the one who lāqaḥ (took, fetched — the same verb as "take a wife" used throughout this chapter) Abraham from his origin-place. The verb connects: the God who took Abraham from Mesopotamia will take a wife from Mesopotamia for Isaac. הוּא יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ (he will send his angel before you) — the divine malʾāḵ going before the servant anticipates Exodus 23:20 ("behold, I am sending an angel before you to guard you on the way"); the Exodus motif of divine angelic escort is here in its patriarchal precursor form. The promise is unconditional: YHWH will act.
8: וְנִקִּיתָ מִשְּׁבֻעָתִי (wĕniqqîtā miššĕḇûʿātî) — "you will be released/clean from my oath." Niqqa (Niphal of nāqāh, to be clean, free, acquitted) — the legal vocabulary of oath-release: if the woman refuses, the servant is legally acquitted from the sworn obligation; the oath's conditions were contingent on the woman's consent. רַק אֶת-בְּנִי לֹא-תָשֵׁב שָׁמָּה — only the returning of Isaac is non-negotiable even within this release clause. The oath's flexibility on the woman is matched by its absolute inflexibility on Isaac remaining in Canaan.
9: וַיָּשֶׂם הָעֶבֶד אֶת-יָדוֹ תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ אַבְרָהָם — the servant performs the oath gesture described in Genesis 24:2. The repetition is the legal confirmation: what was commanded (Genesis 24:2) is now executed (Genesis 24:9). The narrative structure of command → execution without intervening deliberation marks the servant's total obedience — he asked one practical question and received his answer; he then performs the oath without further objection. The oath (wayyiššāḇaʿ lô, he swore to him) is legally complete.
10: עֲשָׂרָה גְמַלִּים (ʿăśārāh ḡĕmallîm, ten camels) — ten camels loaded with gifts is an enormous display of wealth; camels were among the most valuable pack animals of the ANE. Wayyāqom wayyēleḵ ʾel-ʾărammnaharāyim — ʾĂram naharāyim (Aram of the two rivers) = the region of northwest Mesopotamia around the upper Euphrates and Habur rivers, modern southeastern Turkey and northern Syria; this is the Paddan-Aram of the later Jacob narratives (Genesis 28:2). The servant travels from Canaan to this region — a journey of weeks by camel caravan. ʾEl-ʿîr nāḥôr (to the city of Nahor) — the city named after Abraham's brother (Genesis 22:20-24), not necessarily named Nahor but associated with his household.
11: לְעֵת עֶרֶב לְעֵת צֵאת הַשֹּׁאֲבֹת — "at the time of evening, the time when women come out to draw water." The social realism of the well scene: women draw water in the evening when the heat has subsided. The servant positions himself at the well precisely at the time when young women from the town will appear — maximum opportunity for the sign-prayer to be answered. Wayyaḇrēk hagemalîm (he made the camels kneel) — bāraḵ (Hiphil: to cause to kneel; the same root as bĕrāḵāh, blessing — a homonym often noted in rabbinic commentary; the camels kneel as if in blessing-posture before the answer arrives).
12: וַיֹּאמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם הַקְרֵה-נָא לְפָנַי הַיּוֹם — the servant's prayer begins with the divine address "LORD, God of my master Abraham" — he prays in the name of his master's God, not his own (the narrative does not tell us the servant's personal faith status). Haqreh-nāʾ lĕpānay hayyôm — haqreh is the Hiphil imperative of qārāh (to happen, meet, encounter by chance); in the Hiphil: to cause to meet, to arrange a providential encounter. The prayer asks for a divinely managed "chance" meeting — the theology of providence embedded in the verb: what appears random is divinely arranged. וַעֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד עִם אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם (and show ḥeseḏ with my master Abraham) — ḥeseḏ is prayed for Abraham's sake; the servant subordinates his own need to his master's covenant.
13: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם — "behold, I am standing by the spring of water." Niṣṣāḇ (Niphal participle of nāṣaḇ, stationed, positioned, standing firmly) — not merely standing but positioned, planted; the servant has placed himself deliberately at the well. The sign-prayer of Genesis 24:13-14 is one of the most specific petitionary signs in the OT: the servant proposes an exact verbal exchange and a specific action (watering camels) as the divine sign, leaving no room for ambiguity. The specificity is part of the prayer's genre: it asks God to confirm a particular woman through a particular observable action.
14: הוֹכַחְתָּ לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיִצְחָק (hôḵaḥtā lĕʿaḇdĕḵā lĕyiṣḥāq) — "you have designated/decided upon [her] for your servant Isaac." Hôḵaḥtā is the Hiphil perfect of yāḵaḥ (to argue, prove, adjudicate, decide). In the Hiphil the verb can carry the legal force of "to reach a verdict, to determine definitively." The MKT translates "chosen," but the Hiphil nuance goes beyond preference to formal designation: the servant is asking God to reveal his verdict through this observable sign. The prayer essentially invites God to act as a divine arbiter of the marriage choice. וּבָהּ אֵדַע כִּי-עָשִׂיתָ חֶסֶד עִם-אֲדֹנִי (by her I will know that you have done ḥeseḏ with my master) — the sign is the proof of divine action, not a test of the woman.
15: וַיְהִי-הוּא טֶרֶם כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר (wayhî-hûʾ ṭerem killāh lĕḏabbēr) — "before he had finished speaking." Ṭerem (before, not yet) + Piel of kālāh (to finish, complete) = the divine response comes before the prayer is completed. The immediacy is striking: the narrative does not allow even a pause between the petition and the answer; Rebekah's appearance interrupts the prayer mid-utterance. This is the most vivid "before you call, I will answer" moment in the patriarchal narratives (cf. Isaiah 65:24). בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל בֶּן-מִלְכָּה אֵשֶׁת נָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם — the full genealogical identification is inserted by the narrator before Rebekah speaks or the servant knows who she is: the reader sees the answer before the servant does.
16: וְהַנַּעֲרָ טֹבַת מַרְאֶה מְאֹד בְּתוּלָה וְאִישׁ לֹא יְדָעָהּ — "the young woman was very beautiful in appearance, a virgin, and no man had known her." The double specification of virginal status: (1) bĕtûlāh (virgin — the technical term for an unmarried woman who has not had sexual relations; cf. Deuteronomy 22:13-21; Leviticus 21:3; Joel 1:8); and (2) ʾîš lōʾ yĕḏāʿāh (no man had known her — the Qal of yāḏaʿ as sexual knowledge, Genesis 4:1; Genesis 19:8). The redundancy is not careless: it creates legal certainty of her status for the marriage negotiation to follow. Wayyēreḏ hāʿayĕnāh (she went down to the spring) — Rebekah actively goes down to the spring and draws water; her initiative and industriousness appear before any words are spoken.
17: וַיָּרָץ הָעֶבֶד לִקְרָאתָהּ (wayyārāṣ hāʿeḇeḏ liqrāʾtāh) — "the servant ran to meet her." Rûṣ (to run) — the servant's urgency is expressed through running; in the Bible, running typically expresses excitement, hospitality, or urgent response (cf. Abraham's running to greet the divine visitors in Genesis 18:2; Laban's running in Genesis 24:29). הַגְמִיאִינִי נָא (haḡmîʾînî nāʾ) — "please let me sip/drink." Gāmaʾ (to swallow, gulp, sip) — an unusual verb for drinking (more common is šātāh); the Hiphil imperative haḡmîʾînî means "cause me to sip." The specific word choice may emphasize the modesty of the request — just a sip, not a full draw — making Rebekah's generous response (full drink + all the camels) all the more striking.
18: שְׁתֵה אֲדֹנִי (šĕtēh ʾaḏōnî, drink, my lord) — Rebekah's immediate response uses the respectful address ʾaḏōnî (my lord) — appropriate courteous address to a stranger of evident status. וַתְּמַהֵר (wattĕmahēr) — "she hurried." Māhar (to hurry, hasten) — this word will appear multiple times in the chapter (Genesis 24:18, 20, 28, 46) as a verbal motif of Rebekah's energetic character: she is consistently the one who hastens. The hurrying marks the answer to the sign-prayer as immediate and enthusiastic, not merely compliant.
19: גַּם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב עַד אִם-כִּלּוּ לִשְׁתֹּת — "I will also draw for your camels until they have finished drinking." Ad ʾim-killû lištōt (until they have finished drinking) — the scope of the offer is enormous: ten camels can drink 25+ gallons each after a long journey; Rebekah is committing to draw hundreds of gallons of water from a spring. The sign-prayer asked that the woman say exactly these words (Genesis 24:14) — the match is precise. What the servant asked as a hypothetical test is now happening in reality; the prayer is being answered word by word.
20: וַתְּמַהֵר וַתְּעַר כַּדָּהּ אֶל-הַשֹּׁקֶת — "she quickly emptied her jar into the trough." Wattĕmahēr again (Genesis 24:20) — the speed motif continues; she empties, runs (wattārāṣ), draws (wattišʾaḇ) for all the camels. Three rapid verbs in sequence — the narrative's pacing mirrors Rebekah's own energy. Wayyišʾaḇ lĕḵol-gĕmallāyw (she drew enough for all his camels) — the enormity of the work is underscored: not just one camel, not a token draw, but enough for all ten camels to drink their fill after a desert crossing.
21: וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ מַחֲרִישׁ (wĕhāʾîš mištāʾēh lāh maḥărîš) — "the man gazed at her, keeping silent." Mištāʾēh — the Hithpael participle of a verb usually read as "to gaze intently, to be dumbfounded" (from the same root as šāʾāh, to look; or possibly related to Aramaic šĕtā, to look); the Hithpael reflexive implies sustained or absorbed gazing. Maḥărîš (keeping silent, holding back speech) — the servant does not speak, does not react, does not interrupt; he watches in disciplined silence. לָדַעַת הַהִצְלִיחַ יְהוָה דַּרְכּוֹ (to know whether YHWH had prospered his way/journey) — derekh (road, way, journey) — the theological key word of the chapter; the servant's external journey is simultaneously an inner inquiry: has YHWH made this way succeed?
22: נֶזֶם זָהָב בֶּקַע מִשְׁקָלוֹ (nezem zāhāḇ beqaʿ mišqālô) — "a gold nose ring, a beka in weight." Beqaʿ (half-shekel weight) — confirmed in Exodus 38:26 as the weight of the census ransom-half-shekel; its appearance here as a known commercial unit confirms the precision of the transaction. The two bracelets weigh ten shekels of gold — specific weights that match Ugaritic and Nuzi jewelry records for betrothal gifts. The giving of jewelry before the woman's family connection is known (the servant does not yet know who she is, Genesis 24:23) has troubled commentators; some argue the narrator is telescoping; others that the servant acts in confident faith before receiving the verbal confirmation.
23: בַּת-מִי אַתְּ הַגִּידִי נָא לִי (bat-mî ʾatt haggîḏî nāʾ lî) — "whose daughter are you? Please tell me." The servant's first words to Rebekah — after giving the jewelry — are a genealogical inquiry. The social protocol of establishing family connection before negotiating hospitality or marriage is standard ANE practice. הֲיֵשׁ בֵּית-אָבִיךְ מָקוֹם לָנוּ לָלִין (is there room in your father's house for us to spend the night?) — the hospitality inquiry is inseparable from the marriage inquiry; lodging is the physical expression of family openness to the stranger.
24: בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל אָנֹכִי בֶּן-מִלְכָּה אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְנָחוֹר — Rebekah's self-identification: daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, who bore him to Nahor. The genealogical chain is complete: she is Abraham's grand-niece through Nahor (Abraham's brother) and Milcah (Nahor's wife). The servant's mission is accomplished in this one sentence — the woman at the well is of the right family. The genealogy of Genesis 22:20-24 introduced Rebekah precisely for this moment of recognition.
25: גַּם-תֶּבֶן גַּם-מִסְפּוֹא רַב עִמָּנוּ גַּם-מָקוֹם לָלוּן — "we have plenty of straw and fodder, and also room to spend the night." The triple gam (also, moreover) is emphatic accumulation: straw, fodder, lodging — all needs covered without hesitation. Rebekah answers the hospitality inquiry with abundance, not minimal sufficiency. The characterization of Rebekah as generous and quick-to-give is consistent throughout her first appearance: she offers water (Genesis 24:18-20), she answers immediately (Genesis 24:24-25), she will decide immediately on the marriage proposal (Genesis 24:58).
26: וַיִּקֹּד הָאִישׁ וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לַיהוָה — "the man bowed down and prostrated himself before the LORD." The servant's response to Rebekah's genealogical identification is immediate worship — he bows to YHWH before he speaks to Rebekah's household, before he eats, before he negotiates. The reflex of worship is the chapter's theological center: the mission's success is attributed to YHWH, not to the servant's cleverness or Rebekah's beauty. The prostration (wayyišaḥû, Hithpael of šāḥāh) is the full body-to-ground bow of covenant worship.
27: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם — "Blessed be the LORD, God of my master Abraham." The blessing formula (bārûḵ YHWH) is a formal doxology: it does not confer blessing on God but acknowledges and acclaims the blessing that belongs to him. אֲשֶׁר לֹא-עָזַב חַסְדּוֹ וַאֲמִתּוֹ (who has not abandoned his ḥeseḏ and ʾemet) — the divine pair ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet (covenant-loyalty and faithfulness) appears here and in Genesis 24:49 as the theological summary of what YHWH has demonstrated through the providential encounter. This pair will become one of the central divine-character descriptions in the OT (Exodus 34:6; Psalms 25:10; Genesis 61:7; Genesis 89:14; Micah 7:20; and in the NT: John 1:14 "full of grace and truth"). נָחַנִי יְהוָה בְּדֶרֶךְ — "YHWH has led me in the way/road." Nāḥāh (to guide, lead — specifically divine guidance on a path; cf. Psalms 23:3 "he leads me in paths of righteousness"; Psalms 139:24; Isaiah 48:17). The word derekh (way, road) resonates with its literal meaning (the journey) and theological meaning (YHWH's ordered path of providence).
28: וַתָּרָץ הַנַּעֲרָ (wattārāṣ hannāʿărāh) — "the young woman ran." Running again (rûṣ) — Rebekah's fourth expression of energy and initiative; she runs home to tell her mother's household. Bêt ʾimmāh (her mother's household) — the mother's household rather than the father's is the domestic center where such news is first brought; this reflects the household structure of ANE women's social world (cf. Ruth 1:8 where Naomi sends her daughters-in-law back to their mother's house). Bethuel is barely mentioned in the narrative (Genesis 24:50) — Laban and the mother's household are the effective agents of the family response.
29: וּלְרִבְקָה אָח וּשְׁמוֹ לָבָן (ûlĕriḇqāh ʾāḥ ûšĕmô lāḇān) — "Rebekah had a brother and his name was Laban." Lāḇān (white) — the first appearance of a character who will become the dominant figure in the Jacob narrative (Genesis 29-31). His motivation in Genesis 24:30 is immediately revealed: כִּרְאֹתוֹ אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם (when he saw the nose ring) — Laban's action is triggered by seeing the jewelry. The narrative is economical but pointed: he runs out because he saw the jewelry and heard Rebekah's account. The characterization of Laban as materially motivated is established on his first appearance, consistent with his later deceptions with Jacob over wages and inheritance (Genesis 29:25; Genesis 31:7).
30: כִּרְאֹתוֹ אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם וְאֶת-הַצְּמִדִים עַל-יְדֵי אֲחֹתוֹ — the causal clause confirms: Laban's run to the man at the well is motivated by the sight of the nose ring and the bracelets on Rebekah's arms. The narrator does not editorialize further — the observation alone is sufficient characterization. Wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-hāʾîš (he took the man) — the hospitality formula of "taking" the guest home; Laban extends hospitality but the motivation is already disclosed.
31: בֹּא בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה (bōʾ bĕrûḵ YHWH) — "come in, you who are blessed by YHWH." Laban's greeting acknowledges the servant's blessing by YHWH — either a genuine religious acknowledgment or a social formula appropriate to someone whose master is known to be YHWH-blessed. The irony: Laban acknowledges YHWH's blessing on Abraham's servant while his own conduct in the next verse (Genesis 24:30: attracted by the jewelry) reveals a different orientation. Lammāh taʿămod baḥûṣ (why do you stand outside?) — the hospitable urgency of bringing the guest inside; the house and camel space are already prepared.
32: The hospitality hospitality protocol: camels unloaded, straw and fodder provided for the camels, water for the men's foot-washing. Wayyitten mayyim lirhōṣ ragĕlāyw (water was given to wash his feet) — foot-washing on arrival is a standard hospitality marker throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 18:4; Genesis 19:2); it signals the transition from journey to rest. The logical order (camels first, then men) is noted by rabbinic tradition as evidence of the servant's character: he attends to his master's animals before himself.
33: לֹא אֹכַל עַד אִם-דִּבַּרְתִּי דְּבָרָי — "I will not eat until I have spoken my words." The servant refuses food before completing his mission report. The refusal of hospitality before business is transacted is the reverse of typical ANE protocol (eat first, negotiate after) — it signals the urgency of the servant's commission. ʿAdattî (my words, my business, what I have to say) — dāḇār (word, thing, matter) has both meanings; here "my words" = "my business/purpose." The servant's single-mindedness is a character feature: he ran to her (Genesis 24:17), worshipped before eating (Genesis 24:26), and now refuses food before reporting.
34: עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם אָנֹכִי (ʿeḇeḏ ʾaḇrāhām ʾānōḵî) — "I am a servant of Abraham." The servant introduces himself not by his own name but by his master's name — the only self-identification in the chapter. He has no independent identity in this narrative; his purpose is entirely defined by Abraham's commission. The emphatic ʾānōḵî (I, myself) with the predicate "servant of Abraham" is the complete self-presentation: this is who I am, this is what defines me. The anonymity of the servant throughout Genesis 24 is a literary choice: he is the instrument of providence, not its subject.
35: וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אֲדֹנִי מְאֹד — the servant's report to Laban and the household (Genesis 24:34-49) recapitulates Genesis 24:2-27 in virtually exact reported speech. This extended repetition is the chapter's most distinctive literary feature — in most Hebrew narrative, events are summarized when reported; here the servant repeats the conversation nearly verbatim. The effect is theological: the servant's account is not a mere social report but a second witness to divine providence. The exact repetition proves the story's truthfulness: two tellings, one story, one God behind it. The enumeration of Abraham's wealth (sheep, cattle, silver, gold, male and female servants, camels, donkeys) is ANE social positioning: the family will know that the marriage negotiation has real substance.
36: וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנִי בֵן לַאדֹנִי אַחֲרֵי זִקְנָתָהּ — "Sarah, my master's wife, bore a son to my master in her old age." The servant's account of Isaac's birth emphasizes the miraculous element: ʾaḥărê ziqnātāh (after her old age/after she had grown old). The word ziqnāh (old age) is feminine of zāqēn (Genesis 24:1) — the same root. Sarah's giving birth after this stage is presented as the ground for the covenant claims being made: the son of an impossible birth has an inheritance that must be protected by the right marriage. וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ — "he has given him everything he owns" — Isaac is sole heir; the inheritance at stake for the wife-choice is everything Abraham possesses.
37: The servant's restatement of Abraham's prohibition (Genesis 24:37-38 = Genesis 24:3-4) and the conditional oath-release (Genesis 24:41 = Genesis 24:8) in reported speech demonstrates the precision of the commission: he repeats it accurately, including the conditional clauses. The exact verbal repetition in the servant's account is a mark of responsible agency — he is not paraphrasing or adapting his master's instructions but faithfully reporting them. The reliability of the servant as a messenger is part of the narrative's character-building.
38: אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי תֵּלֵךְ (to my father's house you will go) — the servant quotes Abraham's direction using the second person imperative in reported speech, a feature of direct quotation embedded in narrative. The precision of the quotation continues: wĕʾel-mišpaḥtî (and to my clan/family) — mišpāḥāh (clan, extended family unit, kinship group) — the wider circle beyond the immediate household; the woman must come from both the paternal house and the clan/tribe of Abraham's origin.
39: The servant's account of his own hypothetical question (Genesis 24:39 = Genesis 24:5) and Abraham's answer (Genesis 24:40-41 = Genesis 24:7-8) continues the close repetition. The narrative device of repeating the servant's question functions rhetorically in the retelling: it establishes that the servant asked the right practical question, received a theological answer, and then saw the theological answer materialize. The retelling is an argument for divine providence built from the servant's own witness testimony.
40: יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר-הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי לְפָנָיו (YHWH ʾăšer-hithallaḵtî lĕpānāyw) — "the LORD before whom I have walked." Hithallaḵ (Hithpael of hālaḵ, to walk) in the reciprocal-reflexive: "to walk about before/with" — the pattern of Enoch ("Enoch walked with God," Genesis 5:22, 24) and Noah (Genesis 6:9) and the command to Abraham himself (Genesis 17:1: "walk before me and be blameless"). In the servant's retelling, Abraham describes his own covenant walk as the basis for confidence in divine angelic guidance: the God before whom Abraham has walked will guide the servant's steps.
41: תִּנָּקֶה מֵאָלָתִי (tinnāqeh mēʾālātî) — "you will be free/clean from my curse/oath." ʾĀlāh (oath-curse, binding sworn commitment) — the same oath that was šĕḇûʿāh (sworn commitment) in Genesis 24:8 is here called ʾālāh (oath with a self-curse element): oath-oaths in the ANE often involved an imprecation — "may X happen to me if I fail." The release formula tinnāqeh (Niphal: you will be acquitted, made clean) uses the legal vocabulary of innocence.
42: The servant's account of his arrival at the spring and his prayer (Genesis 24:42-44 = Genesis 24:12-14) continues the close repetition. One word difference is notable: in the original prayer (Genesis 24:12) the servant asks haqreh-nāʾ lĕpānay (cause to meet before me); in the retelling (Genesis 24:42) he says ʾim-yešĕḵā-nāʾ maṣlîaḥ darkî (if you are now making my way succeed). The retelling reformulates the prayer in retrospective terms — he is now reporting to the family what he prayed, not repeating the prayer itself; the slight variation is natural in oral retelling and not a textual inconsistency.
43: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם — the servant quotes his own sign-prayer to the household (Genesis 24:43 = Genesis 24:13). The repetition of the prayer's exact terms in the retelling accomplishes a rhetorical goal: when the servant then describes Rebekah's appearance and actions (Genesis 24:45-47), the match between what he prayed for and what happened is visible and precise to the household. They are being invited to draw the same conclusion he drew — this is from the LORD (Genesis 24:50).
44: The sign's verbal specification (Genesis 24:44 = Genesis 24:14): the woman must offer to draw water for the camels. The household, hearing both the prayer and its fulfillment, is presented with an irrefutable argument: the exact words, the exact action, the exact family lineage — "this is from the LORD" (Genesis 24:50) is the only conclusion the evidence supports. The servant's retelling is a piece of theological argument dressed in narrative form.
45: אֲנִי טֶרֶם אֲכַלֶּה לְדַבֵּר אֶל-לִבִּי (ʾănî ṭerem ʾăkalleh lĕḏabbēr ʾel-libbî) — "before I finished speaking to my heart." The phrase lĕḏabbēr ʾel-libbî (speaking to my heart) — the servant clarifies that the prayer of Genesis 24:12-14 was an interior prayer, not voiced aloud to Rebekah or anyone else. The prayer was between the servant and YHWH alone; Rebekah's response was unprompted by any human request she could have heard. This detail confirms the sign's divine character: Rebekah cannot have overheard the prayer she was answering.
46: The servant's account of Rebekah's actions (Genesis 24:46 = Genesis 24:18-20): she lowered her jar, gave him a drink, watered the camels. The match is exact. The narrative economy of the retelling shows what the servant noticed and retained: the specific verbal exchange, the specific actions. He has been watching closely (Genesis 24:21: he watched in silence to know if YHWH had prospered his way) and can now report precisely. The family receives a testimony that is both exact and first-hand.
47: וָאֶשְׁאַל אֹתָהּ וָאֹמַר בַּת-מִי אַתְּ — "I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?'" The servant's account of the genealogical inquiry (Genesis 24:47 = Genesis 24:23). One detail differs from the earlier narrative: in Genesis 24:22-23, the jewelry is given before the genealogical question; in Genesis 24:47, the servant says he asked the genealogical question first and then gave the jewelry after she answered. This apparent discrepancy is either: (1) a narrative telescoping where the order in the first telling is approximate; (2) a deliberate variation showing that the servant tidied the sequence in his retelling for social reasons (to avoid admitting he gave jewelry to an unknown woman); (3) a natural feature of oral narration where slight reorderings occur in retelling. The discrepancy is noted in both Jewish and Christian commentary; it does not affect the theological argument.
48: וָאֶקֹּד וָאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לַיהוָה (I bowed and prostrated myself before YHWH) — the worship moment (Genesis 24:26-27) is reported in the retelling: the servant's immediate response to the genealogical confirmation was worship. ʾĂšer hiḏrîḵanî bĕdereḵ ʾemet (who led me in the way of truth) — bĕdereḵ ʾemet (in the way of truth/faithfulness) — ʾemet (truth, faithfulness, reliability) modifying the journey; the same word that appears in the divine-character pair ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet. The servant has walked in the reliable way that YHWH prepared for him — the word ʾemet connects the servant's journey to the divine character it manifested.
49: וְעַתָּה אִם-יֶשְׁכֶם עֹשִׂים חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת (wĕʿattāh ʾim-yeškem ʿōśîm ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet) — "and now, if you are doing ḥeseḏ and ʾemet." The servant now asks the family to do for Abraham what YHWH has already demonstrated: ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet (covenant-loyalty and faithfulness). The theological pair is applied first to God (Genesis 24:27: YHWH has not abandoned his ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet) and now to the human family (Genesis 24:49: will you do ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet?). The family is invited to imitate the divine character in their response. Waʾim-lāʾ haggîḏû lî wĕʾepĕneh ʿal-yāmîn ô ʿal-śĕmōʾl (if not, tell me so I may turn to the right or the left) — the servant requests a clear answer; ambiguity is not acceptable.
50: מֵיְהוָה יָצָא הַדָּבָר (mêYHWH yāṣāʾ haddāḇār) — "from YHWH this thing has gone forth." Laban and Bethuel's response is the theological climax of the servant's account: they recognize the divine hand in the sequence of events and cannot oppose it. Lōʾ nûḵal dabbēr ʾēleḵā rāʿ ô-ṭôḇ (we cannot say to you bad or good) — the idiom of "bad or good" (rāʿ ô-ṭôḇ) means something like "anything at all, either way" — they have no counterargument. The confession is remarkable: pagan Arameans from Nahor's family acknowledge YHWH's action and submit to it. The narrative presents them as recognizing divine providence when they see it.
51: הִנֵּה-רִבְקָה לְפָנֶיךָ קַח וָלֵךְ (hinnēh-riḇqāh lĕpānêḵā qaḥ wālēḵ) — "behold, Rebekah is before you, take her and go." Three imperatives in rapid sequence — qaḥ (take), wālēḵ (go), and implicitly hinnēh (behold, here she is). The family's consent is given immediately and completely, without conditions or further negotiation. Wattĕhî ʾiššāh lĕḇen-ʾăḏōnêḵā kaʾăšer dibber YHWH (let her become the wife of your master's son, as YHWH has spoken) — the final clause anchors the family's decision in divine speech: they are not giving Rebekah but fulfilling YHWH's word.
52: וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לָאָרֶץ לַיהוָה — "he prostrated himself to the ground before YHWH." The servant's worship response on hearing their consent mirrors his worship response on hearing Rebekah's genealogy (Genesis 24:26). The pattern is consistent throughout the chapter: news of divine action → immediate worship. The servant's theology is straightforward and practical: when YHWH acts, you bow.
53: כְּלֵי-כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּבְגָדִים (silver articles and gold articles and garments) — the betrothal gifts flow from the servant's treasury to Rebekah, her brother, and her mother. The distribution pattern is socially precise: the bride receives jewelry; the brother and mother receive gifts as the household gatekeepers who consented to the marriage. ûlĕʾāḥîhā ûlĕʾimmāh (to her brother and to her mother) — notably not to Bethuel the father; the mother and Laban are the effective household agents throughout this chapter. The gift-giving formalizes the betrothal.
54: וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֻנִי לַאדֹנִי (wayyōʾmer šallĕḥûnî laʾḏōnî) — "send me on my way to my master." Šāllaḥ (to send, release, dismiss) — the same verb repeatedly used for the sending of the servant on his mission. Now the servant asks to be released back to his master — mission complete, account given, gifts distributed, consent secured. The next morning arrives and his first words are return-focused. The servant's entire being is oriented toward Abraham and the commission; even after the celebration meal he thinks only of returning.
55: יֵשֵׁב הַנַּעֲרָ אִתָּנוּ יָמִים אוֹ עָשׂוֹר (yēšēḇ hannāʿărāh ʾittānû yāmîm ô ʿāśôr) — "let the young woman remain with us ten days or so." Yāmîm ô ʿāśôr (days or ten) — the idiom probably means "about ten days" or "a period of days or approximately ten." The family asks for a customary transition period before releasing Rebekah into marriage and departure — the request is understandable and socially appropriate. The servant's refusal in Genesis 24:56 is not social rudeness but commission-urgency: YHWH has granted success; delay introduces risk. The underlying theology: once God has acted, human delay is a form of hesitancy toward the divine will.
56: אַל-תְּאַחֲרוּ אֹתִי (ʾal-tĕʾahărû ʾōtî) — "do not delay me." ʾAḥar (Piel: to delay, hold back, cause to tarry) — the servant's refusal is stated as a negative command: stop delaying me. וַיהוָה הִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי (and YHWH has granted success to my way/journey) — the servant's entire theological framework is explicit: YHWH has acted, the mission is complete, lingering serves no purpose. The word derekh appears again — the servant's journey-as-theological-event is concluded. The urgency is not impatience but faith: the God who has acted should not be kept waiting by human social convention.
57: נִקְרָא לַנַּעֲרָ וְנִשְׁאֲלָה אֶת-פִּיהָ (niqrāʾ lannāʿărāh wĕnišʾălāh ʾet-pîhā) — "let us call the young woman and ask her mouth/ask her directly." ʾEt-pîhā (her mouth, literally) — the idiom "ask her mouth" = consult her directly, ask her in person. The family's suggestion to ask Rebekah directly is notable: the woman's consent is sought. In ANE marriage practice, the woman's family arranged marriages; direct consultation of the bride was not always required. Here the family recognizes that Rebekah herself must decide — an unusual degree of female agency in the narrative, but consistent with her active, decisive characterization throughout the chapter.
58: הֲתֵלְכִי עִם-הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה (hătēlĕḵî ʿim-hāʾîš hazzeh) — "will you go with this man?" The question is direct: a yes/no choice put to Rebekah. וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלֵךְ (wattōʾmer ʾēlēḵ) — "she said: I will go." ʾĒlēḵ — the Qal imperfect first-person singular: "I will go, I myself will go." One word — decisive, immediate, final. The character of Rebekah crystallized in a single verb: the woman who runs, who waters the camels, who answers immediately, now says "I will go" to the most consequential question of her life. The narrative does not describe hesitation, consultation, or qualification — just ʾēlēḵ.
59: וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת-רִבְקָה אֲחֹתָם — "they sent away Rebekah their sister." Šāllaḥ again — the verb of sending that has moved through the whole chapter: Abraham sent (šāllaḥ) the servant (Genesis 24:7); the servant asked to be sent back (Genesis 24:54, 56); now the family sends (wayyĕšallĕḥû) Rebekah. The sending vocabulary threads the narrative together: a commission is given, a mission is executed, a woman is sent — all with the same verb. אֶת-מֵינֶקֶת (her nurse) — Rebekah travels with her nurse; in 35:8 this nurse is named Deborah, who dies at Beth-el and is mourned under the "oak of weeping." The nurse's inclusion in the household is a mark of status.
60: אֲחֹתֵנוּ אַתְּ הֲיִי לְאַלְפֵי רְבָבָה (ʾăḥōtēnû ʾatt hăyî lĕʾalpê rĕḇāḇāh) — "our sister, may you become thousands of ten-thousands." The blessing formula: ʾalpê rĕḇāḇāh (thousands of ten-thousands, literally) — innumerability expressed by combining two large-number words. This echoes and extends the Abrahamic promise vocabulary: "I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars" (Genesis 15:5; Genesis 22:17 — "stars of heaven and sand on the seashore"). The family blessing on Rebekah uses the same register as the divine promise to Abraham. וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו (may your offspring possess the gate of their enemies) — identical language to the post-Akedah covenant oath in Genesis 22:17: "your offspring will possess the gate of his enemies." The family pronounces a blessing that repeats YHWH's own sworn promise — whether they know this or not, the narrator does.
61: וַתָּקָם רִבְקָה וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ (wattāqom riḇqāh wĕnaʿărōtehā) — "Rebekah and her young women rose up." The departure scene is economical: they rise, mount the camels, go with the man. Wayyiqqaḥ hāʿeḇeḏ ʾet-riḇqāh wayyēleḵ (the servant took Rebekah and left) — the mission concludes with the same verb (lāqaḥ, to take) used for Abraham's instruction to "take a wife" for Isaac (Genesis 24:3-4). The commission is fulfilled: the servant took (from Mesopotamia) what Abraham commanded him to take (a wife for Isaac).
62: וְיִצְחָק בָּא מִבּוֹא בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי (wĕyiṣḥāq bāʾ mibôʾ bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî) — "and Isaac came from going to Beer-lahai-roi." Bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî (the well of the Living One who sees me) — the well named by Hagar in 16:14 after the divine encounter that promised Ishmael's greatness. Isaac's presence at Hagar's well is an ironic narrative detail: the covenant heir comes from the place associated with the expelled half-brother. The final section of the chapter (Genesis 24:62-67) shifts to Isaac's perspective — he has been absent from the narrative since the Akedah (Genesis 22); now he reappears for his marriage. wĕhûʾ yôšēḇ bĕʾereṣ hannèḡeḇ (and he was living in the Negev) — the southern desert region where Beersheba is located.
63: וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִפְנוֹת עָרֶב — "Isaac went out to lāśûaḥ in the field in the evening." Lāśûaḥ — the key hapax of the verse. The verb śûaḥ (or śîaḥ) appears in Job 7:11; Psalms 77:4 (to muse/complain) and Psalms 105:2 (to meditate); the root śîaḥ as a noun means "meditation, reflection, complaint, speech." The Vulgate translates meditari (to meditate); the LXX ἀδολεσχῆσαι (to meditate, muse). The precise meaning of the verb in this context is deliberately ambiguous — the reader cannot be certain whether Isaac is meditating, praying, walking in reflection, or simply strolling. The word creates a moment of interior space before the meeting: Isaac is in a contemplative state when Rebekah arrives.
64: וַתִּשָּׂא רִבְקָה אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא אֶת-יִצְחָק — "Rebekah lifted up her eyes and saw Isaac." The mutual seeing: Isaac first appears in Rebekah's view (she sees him); then Rebekah appears to Isaac (he sees her, Genesis 24:63: "as he looked up, he saw camels approaching"). The narrative orders the seeing to give Rebekah the first active role: she sees the man she is about to marry. Wattippol mēʿal haggāmāl (she fell/dismounted from the camel) — the verb nāpal (to fall) is used idiomatically for dismounting; the action is deferential, appropriate when approaching a figure of evident significance.
65: מִי-הָאִישׁ הַלָּזֶה הַהֹלֵךְ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִקְרָאתֵנוּ — "who is that man walking in the field to meet us?" Rebekah asks the servant. Hāʾîš hazzeh (this man) — her question uses lāzeh, an unusual demonstrative form (found only in later texts: Genesis 37:19; Judges 18:4; 1 Samuel 21:16 — possibly archaic or dialectal). הוּא אֲדֹנִי (hûʾ ʾăḏōnî, he is my master) — the servant's answer gives Isaac his title before his name; in calling Isaac ʾăḏōnî (my master), the servant signals the transfer of authority: the servant who has called Abraham ʾăḏōnî (my master) throughout now applies the same term to Isaac — the covenant has passed to the next generation. וַתִּקַּח הַצָּעִיף וַתִּתְכָּס (she took the veil and covered herself) — the veil is the modesty/bridal gesture appropriate to the first meeting of husband and wife; it signals Rebekah's recognition of the situation and her acceptance of her new status.
66: וַיְסַפֵּר הָעֶבֶד לְיִצְחָק אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה — "the servant told Isaac all the things he had done." The servant's account to Isaac is the third telling of the chapter's events: first the events themselves (Genesis 24:10-27), then the servant's report to Laban's household (Genesis 24:34-49), now a brief mention of the report to Isaac. The third telling is not narrated in detail — the narrator summarizes it in one verse. The narrative economy at the end contrasts with the extended repetition in the middle: what needed proving to Laban's household is simply confirmed to Isaac.
67: וַיְבִאֶהָ יִצְחָק הָאֹהֱלָה שָׂרָה אִמּוֹ (wayyĕḇîʾehā yiṣḥāq hāʾōhĕlāh śārāh ʾimmô) — "Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother." The tent of Sarah (ʾōhel śārāh) — the definite article makes it specific: this is the tent, the known household of the covenant matriarch who died in Genesis 23. The marriage is situated within Sarah's tent, connecting Rebekah directly to Sarah's household role. וַיֶּאֱהָבֶהָ (wayyeʾĕhāḇehā) — "and he loved her." The second use of the verb ʾāhab (to love) in Genesis — the first was Genesis 22:2, where God said "your son, your only son, whom you love" (using ʾāhab for Abraham's love for Isaac). Now ʾāhab is used for Isaac's love for Rebekah: the covenant line is preserved not merely through legal arrangement but through genuine love. וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ (wayyinnāḥem yiṣḥāq ʾaḥărê ʾimmô) — "and Isaac was comforted after his mother." Nāḥam (Niphal: to be comforted, to receive comfort — the same root as Noah's name, Genesis 5:29: "this one will comfort us"; and the name Nahum). Isaac's grief over Sarah's death (Genesis 23) is resolved by Rebekah: marriage and love are the form comfort takes in the covenant narrative.
